confidence_rationale: Niv 2009 is the canonical review linking model-free reinforcement learning (temporal difference, TD) to dopaminergic prediction error signals in the striatum (SNc/VTA -> striatum axis) and model-based planning to prefrontal-hippocampal circuitry. The paper rigorously grounds the distinction between cached value learning (TD/model-free, habit) and model-based planning in neural substrates. It provides the computational scaffolding for MECH-163's dual-system claim. Confidence is moderate-high because the review focuses primarily on the model-free/TD side of the architecture with the model-based planning arm less developed; the specific E3 hippocampal multi-step harm evaluation is not addressed. The paper predates the clearer dorsomedial/dorsolateral striatum dissociation literature (Balleine & O'Doherty 2010) and does not foreground the hippocampal contribution.
failure_signatures: Niv 2009 primarily reviews the TD/model-free arm; model-based planning is discussed but less developed relative to subsequent literature.
The hippocampal contribution to multi-step trajectory planning is not the focus of this review; hippocampus appears primarily in the context of episodic memory rather than action planning.
The paper does not address harm attribution or ethical/prosocial planning dimensions of MECH-163.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Temporal difference (TD) prediction errors, signalled by phasic dopamine neurons in SNc/VTA projecting to striatum, implement model-free reinforcement learning in the brain. The brain also contains model-based systems involving prefrontal cortex that can plan over an internal model of the environment. These two systems co-exist and interact.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-163 maps directly onto this architecture. The SNc -> dorsal striatum dopamine axis implements the REE habit system (model-free S-R caching). The VTA -> ventral striatum + prefrontal/hippocampal system implements the planned system (model-based). Niv 2009's TD framing is the computational basis for the habit system's inability to attribute harm across novel contexts: TD systems learn cached values without access to the transition model, making contextual harm attribution (which requires simulating counterfactual trajectories) inaccessible to them. The REE E3 hippocampal system's multi-step trajectory evaluation corresponds to the model-based planning arm Niv describes.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Niv 2009 provides the computational grounding for the model-free arm of MECH-163 far more precisely than the model-based arm. The specific claim that model-based planning requires hippocampal trajectory generation (not just PFC) requires reading Niv in conjunction with hippocampal planning literature (e.g., Balleine & O'Doherty 2010, Dolan & Dayan 2013). The harm-attribution dimension of MECH-163 is an REE-specific framing not present in Niv.
mapping.source_context: Comprehensive review across rodent electrophysiology, human fMRI, and computational modelling. Journal of Mathematical Psychology -- focused on formal/computational framing rather than clinical application.

confidence_components.notes: Niv 2009 is a highly cited, technically rigorous review in a leading computational journal. Source quality is very high. Mapping fidelity is good for the model-free/TD side and the dual-system co-existence claim, but the model-based/hippocampal side is underdeveloped relative to what MECH-163 requires. Transfer risk is low-moderate: the computational claim translates cleanly; the specific hippocampal harm-attribution extension is an REE addition.
